items.q: Which macronutrient provides the most energy per gram?
items.why: Students assume carbs (the 'energy food') provide most energy per gram. Carbs and protein = 4 kcal/g; FAT = 9 kcal/g (most energy-dense). Vitamins provide no energy at all.

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.
Students confuse fat- and water-soluble vitamins. Mnemonic: ADEK fat-soluble (stored in body fat - can build up to toxic levels). B and C water-soluble (excess excreted in urine).

items.correct: No - gluten is a protein formed in wheat flour when flour proteins mix with water, giving dough its stretch.
items.misconception: Yes - gluten is the starchy carbohydrate part of bread.
items.random_wrong: Gluten is a type of fat.
items.why: Because gluten lives in bread and pasta, students file it under 'carbs'. It is actually the elastic PROTEIN network (formed from glutenin and gliadin when flour is hydrated and kneaded) that traps gas bubbles and gives bread its chewy structure.

items.q: Is sweetening the only job sugar does in baking?
items.correct: No - sugar also helps browning, keeps products moist, acts as a preservative, and helps trap air when creamed with fat.
items.misconception: Yes - sugar is only there for sweetness and could be removed without changing anything else.
items.random_wrong: Sugar's main job is to add protein.
items.why: Cutting sugar from a recipe changes more than taste: less browning (caramelisation/Maillard), a drier and tougher crumb, shorter shelf life, and poorer aeration in creamed cakes. Sugar is a multi-functional ingredient, which is why reformulating is hard.

items.q: Which ingredient mainly provides structure to bread?
items.why: Correct answer: Flour. “Sugar” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Sugar acts as a preservative in jam by:
items.correct: Binding water so microbes can't grow
items.why: It's Binding water so microbes can't grow. “Killing all bacteria instantly” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many roles for sugar are listed in this lesson?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
